ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents supervised-learning models for sentiment quantification on app reviews and tweets but does not describe any novel algorithmic idea or method—authors state “It is not possible to give more details on the engine due to non-disclosure restrictions.” The task (sentiment analysis/quantification) is well-studied and the work mainly reports empirical comparisons via the existing iFeel platform and SentiStrengthIta on bespoke app/tweet datasets. Given the lack of disclosed methodological innovation or clear differentiation from prior supervised/NLP approaches, the contribution appears to mostly combine known techniques with limited novelty.

CLARITY.justification: The manuscript is poorly organized and contains multiple repetitions, missing subsections (e.g. jump from 2.1 to 2.3) and duplicated/garbled paragraphs (several repeated sentences and broken reference citations), which disrupt logical flow. Key methodological details are withheld (“not possible to give more details…due to non-disclosure”), and while the sentiment scoring scheme is described, there is inconsistent presentation of experiments (tables are referenced but not shown in the provided text). Overall the writing has grammatical and formatting issues (line breaks, incomplete references), so substantial editing and reorganization are needed for clarity.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (sentiment analysis for tweets and app reviews) is squarely within NLP/ML scope and the paper targets the right audience by benchmarking against research tools and using standard metrics/datasets (e.g. macro F1, SemEval-related tweet sets). However, the core methods are undisclosed (“It is not possible to give more details on the engine due to non-disclosure restrictions”), which weakens reproducibility and scientific contribution expected at top-tier venues. Evaluation practice is reasonable (multiple test sets, comparisons via iFeel) but the lack of methodological detail and apparent emphasis on a commercial tool/demo make this a borderline submission rather than a clear fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

REPLICABILITY.justification: The paper explicitly withholds model/engine details (“It is not possible to give more details on the engine due to non-disclosure restrictions”), so hyperparameters, architectures and training configurations are not provided. The preprocessing pipeline is not described and training data is only vaguely characterized as a “huge variety of language domains” (no training corpora or splits specified), although test sets are partially described (A-ita/A-eng 1k Candy Crush comments, B sets of 10k across apps, and tweet sets from Araújo et al.). Code is not provided and methods cannot be reimplemented from the description; the only clear reproducible element is the evaluation metrics (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1) and that test benchmarks are claimed to be submitted with the paper, which is insufficient for full replication.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs evaluations on multiple benchmarks (A-ita/A-eng 1k annotated Candy Crush sets, a 10k multi-app Test set B, 3899 Italian and 1000 English tweets) and compares App2Check/Tweet2Check against 19 tools via iFeel and SentiStrengthIta, which gives reasonable coverage of test data and baselines. However, critical methodological details are withheld (“not possible to give more details on the engine due to non-disclosure restrictions”), there are no ablation or sensitivity analyses, no error/failure-case discussion, no statistical significance testing, and insufficient information on training data, hyperparameters or reproducibility despite offering a demo—overall making the experimental validation shallow and hard to reproduce.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses a relevant practical problem—sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets in both Italian and English—and provides evaluated tools (App2Check/Tweet2Check) against 19 competitors using the iFeel platform and multiple benchmarks (Section 3), which is valuable for practitioners. The authors claim their models outperform state-of-the-art research tools on app reviews and tweets and supply the benchmark datasets and demo access, increasing immediate usability and potential adoption (Conclusion; Experimental Evaluation). However, the opaque engine ("not possible to give more details on the engine due to non-disclosure restrictions") and lack of novel methodological insights limit broader research impact and new directions, making the contribution useful but not field-changing.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper reports comparative experiments on several benchmark sets (A-ita/A-eng labeled by a single trained annotator, B with 10k app comments, and tweet sets) and claims App2Check/Tweet2Check "outperform" competitors, but gives crucial methodological details missing or unclear: the predictive engine is withheld ("non-disclosure restrictions"), training data and feature/model choices are unspecified, and it is not stated whether any test/validation leakage or overlap with training data occurred. The annotation and ground-truth for the B testset are ambiguous (they merely state each comment has an app rating but do not clarify if that was used as HSQ), only one annotator is reported for A-sets, and no statistical significance tests or detailed evaluation tables are provided in the excerpt. These gaps in reproducibility, possible labeling/overlap issues and lack of statistical analysis constitute significant technical weaknesses that undermine the strength of the stated conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper does compare against a large set of baselines (19 research tools via the iFeel platform and SentiStrengthIta) on shared datasets and reports standard metrics (macro F1, per-class F1, accuracy) —see Sec. 2.1 and 3 describing iFeel, the test sets and metrics. However, important details are missing: the exact list/configuration of the 19 tools is not given in the excerpt, the authors do not disclose their engine or training data (non‑disclosure), and it is unclear whether baselines were retrained or used off‑the‑shelf (potential unfairness). The paper also offers little analysis of when prior methods perform comparably or better or why the proposed models win, and does not provide detailed error analysis or discussion of specific competing approaches beyond iFeel/SentiStrength, limiting the depth of the comparison.
